attributes.stem:
A 40-year-old woman is seen in the Emergency Department with gradual onset of epigastric pain. She has been unwell for 12 hours, feels nauseated but has not vomited. Her blood pressure is 130/90 mmHg with a pulse rate of 80/min and a temperature of 37.3ºC. She is tender in the epigastrium. An ultrasound examination performed one month earlier had shown gallstones.

Blood tests reveal:

  • Lipase: 700 U/L (normal <60)

  • Bilirubin: 10umol/L (2-24)

  • ALP: 150U/L ( 30-110)

  • GGT 50U/L (0-60)

  • AST 30U/L (0-45)

  • ALT 50U/L (0-45)

Her general condition improves considerably over the first 24 hours and she is now relatively pain-free.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has presented with acute pancreatitis. Previous investigations have confirmed the presence of gallstones and she now has a significantly elevated serum lipase. The two most common causes of acute pancreatitis are alcohol and gallstones. Gallstone obstruction is the most common cause of acute pancreatitis (termed biliary pancreatitis) accounting for 35-75% of cases. Traditionally, after the initial presentation of biliary pancreatitis, 25-76% of patients will present with recurrent attacks. The only definitive treatment for acute biliary pancreatitis is laparoscopic cholecystectomy, which reduces the risk of recurrent biliary pancreatitis to almost zero. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy is a minimally invasive surgical procedure. Therefore, current Australian guidelines of management of acute pancreatitis indicate that patients with mild biliary pancreatitis should have a laparoscopic cholecystectomy on their initial admission to hospital once their pain has settled. Hence this should be the plan of action for this patient.

As gallstones have already been imaged, a CT of the abdomen is only indicated during initial assessment if there is doubt of the clinical diagnosis. Pancreatitis is diagnosed when 2 of 3 criteria are present: clinical, biochemical or radiological. This patient has a history consistent with pancreatitis and elevated lipase, therefore imaging (i.e. CT to see pancreatitis) is not required. Performance of a CT scan in a patient with pancreatitis is usually reserved for cases where a complication (eg pancreatic necrosis) is suspected.

A repeat ultrasound would be indicated if there was obstructive LFT derangement, to determine if there was a stone obstructing the biliary system that would require ERCP. Recent research has shown that early endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) does not have an impact on patient mortality and does not reduce complications, except when performed in patients with biliary pancreatitis and concurrent cholangitis or common bile duct obstruction. There is no evidence that this patient has common bile duct obstruction. Current evidence suggests that ERCP is rarely necessary in cases of gallstone-related pancreatitis as most stones are small and pass spontaneously.

Therefore, an ERCP would not be the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient. The essentially normal LFTs and normal bilirubin suggest there is no duct obstruction, therefore there is no need for an ERCP to visualise the biliary tree. The patient will have duct visualisation (operative choledochography) performed as part of the cholecystectomy procedure and the presence of any stones identified. If a stone is found in the biliary tree, it could either be extracted during the surgical procedure or by post-operative ERCP.

A repeat lipase blood test would not be necessary in this patient as a diagnosis of acute pancreatitis can be made using the first serum lipase result. Serum lipase rises within 4-8 hours after the onset of symptoms, and remains increased for 8-14 days, therefore, repeat testing would not provide any clinical benefit to the management of this patient.
learningPoints: Mild episodes of acute biliary pancreatitis should be managed with early laparoscopic cholecystectomy as it is the only definitive treatment that has shown to maximally reduce the risk of recurrent attacks.
Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) only reduces mortality and morbidity in patients with biliary pancreatitis and concurrent cholangitis or common bile duct obstruction.
references: Liu C, Lo C, Fan S. Acute biliary pancreatitis: Diagnosis and management. World Journal of Surgery. 1997;21(2):149-154. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8995070/

Nesvaderani M, Eslick G, Cox M. Acute pancreatitis: update on management. Medical Journal of Australia. 2015;202(8):420-423. Available at: https://www.mja.com.au/journal/2015/202/8/acute-pancreatitis-update-management

Trust M, Sheffield K, Boyd C, Benarroch-Gampel J, Zhang D, Townsend C et al. Gallstone pancreatitis in older patients: Are we operating enough?. Surgery. 2011;150(3):515-525. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3353416/
McDermott J, Kao LS, Keeley JA, et al. Management of Gallstone Pancreatitis A Review. JAMA Surg. 2024; 159:818-25
